reviews.review_text:
Fav hitman game yet since i cant run WOA, might not have similar attention to details as bloodmoney yet the overall combat has imrpoved drastically , absolution is the perfect transition between the newer games and the legacy games with the introduction of challenges , instinct and throwing objects, also this still goes on huge sale while also having achivements, althought you cant complete some due to contacts gamemode being removed.
The missions are a bit linear not providing enough freedom but there are still multiple ways to end the target..

Booting up Absolution again feels like stepping into a time capsule of 2012 cinematic gaming taking me back to my high school gaming days. The vibe of this game is unmatched. It doesn't have the clean, sterile, high-society aesthetic of the modern World of Assassination trilogy. Instead, Absolution is drenched in sweat, rain, neon lights, and a deeply cynical, over the top grindhouse film energy. The stylized, high-contrast lighting and bloom effects scream early 2010s in the best way possible. Is it a traditional Hitman game? No. The disguise system is famously broken where every single person in the same uniform instantly detects you unless you burn through your instinct meter. But judged on its own merits as a dark, stylish, linear action stealth thriller, it is a phenomenal experience that they simply don't make anymore.  Lastly, coming back to it feels like visiting an old, chaotic friend. For the pure joy of the atmosphere, the punchy mechanics, and the sheer 2012 nostalgia, it’s a flawless trip down memory lane.

Good game, just not what you would expect from Hitman, it's more linear but it's still worth a playthrough, also the cutscenes are hilarious fever dreams (limp ♥♥♥♥ lenny!) and every villain is comically evil.

It masterfully follows the story left from Hitman Blood Money, with an improved experience under every aspect of the game.
Even more challenging, and so even more rewarding!

Hitman: Absolution is the first game I played in the Hitman franchise. It's not the best game in the series, but it's definitely not the worst either. I really enjoyed the strong focus on the story, although I wish they had included more ways to eliminate enemies like they did in some of the previous games. I also liked how much emotion Agent 47 shows in this story compared to the other games.

Sometimes the game can feel frustrating because you're trying to do things that are quite difficult without using all of your abilities, but become extremely easy when you do use them. Because of that, the gameplay can feel a little unbalanced at times. This game isn't everyone's cup of tea, but I personally loved it. I especially liked how some missions let you completely cheese the objectives and just have fun experimenting.

The best part of the game, however, is the voice acting. Oh my God, it was so damn good. Overall, Hitman: Absolution may not be the strongest game in the franchise, but it's one that I had a great time playing and would gladly recommend to fans of story-driven action games.
this game never fails to deliver iv played through my childhood  and had to replay over all a 10 out of 10

I'm playing the series in release order, each game for the first time except for Silent Assassin and Contracts, both of which I played around 2010-2012. So far, I think each Hitman game is overall better than the previous one. In my opinion, the franchise reached its best atmosphere in Contracts, but other than that I feel like Absolution is where Hitman finally feels like a complete experience rather than a bundle of missions with a bit of story sprinkled on top. Also, I like how 47 gets to shine a bit more as a character with each sequel, as opposed to the borderline blank avatar he is in the first game.

The first Hitman game I've clicked with.

Decent story, OK voice acting, stealth and combat is excellent.

It's nice to see the sentiment around this game has completely done a 180. I remember how excited I was when it launched, only to see people everywhere tearing it apart. A lot of the criticism came from the fact that it lacked the pure sandbox freedom that Blood Money was known for. But honestly, for the story Absolution was trying to tell, that structure wouldn't have worked nearly as well.

If I had one major complaint, it's the Instinct system. Almost every guard can see through almost any disguise, forcing you to spend Instinct just to avoid getting caught. In theory, I understand the reasoning: everyone knows who you are and is actively hunting you, so you're no longer the invisible ghost Agent 47 usually is. In practice, though, it can be frustrating and sometimes makes disguises feel less meaningful. Even so, it's not enough to ruin the experience for me.

Everything else is wonderfully executed. The atmosphere of this game is incredibly distinct and unlike anything else in the series. Whether it's the presentation, the tone, or the world itself, Absolution has a unique identity that has only grown on me over the years.

I highly recommend giving it a chance if you've never played it. It's a very nostalgic game for me, and a lot of great memories are tied to it.
This was my first hitman game and I AM HOOKED. The game runs well on my krappy laptop, the gameplay is fun, and the story is amazing!

My first Hitman experience.

The game expects you to have the memory of a supercomputer and the stealth skills of a ghost. It’s perfect if you love sneaking around and somehow keeping track of where every single AI is standing at all times like you’re running a mental GPS system. I watched Jolumz platinum it and thought, “Yeah, I can do that,” only to discover this version’s online servers have been deleted like they packed their bags and left years ago. Even with that surprise, it was still a really good time,  just me, my questionable memory, and a bunch of NPCs who deserved better.
10/10 would still replay.

Pretty satisfying action and a few pretty memorable levels. Graphics hold up surprisingly well on PC still.

The step of old the system of Hitman to the new that we see in more recent releases has both charm, and frustration. It's not a bad game, but it's potential isn't realised due to a lack in level design, an obscene amount of enemies, clunky controls, and a lack of feedback when it comes to restricted areas.

Older games would have a guard hold out their hand and say "Sorry sir, you can't go past here". Nice and easy. Absolution doesn't have this. You will be trespassing very quickly and things can go really south very quickly. The world also reacts...awkwardly. Sometimes a gunshot will be heard, sometimes it won't. Sometimes your instinct allows you to blend into your surroundings...sometimes it doesn't. The game kinda just does it's own thing at times which can add to the frustrations.

The levels do feel quite open, but often there are just so many enemies that it's more about finding that one path around them leading to the game feeling very linear at times, and some objectives really need additional ways of getting to them (something that would addressed in late releases). Just be warned, while each level is fairly short, these levels can get really long if you aren't guns blazing, and restart levels and chapters to try and be as silent as possible.

That being said, things like hiding bodies, using the environment to create accidents, playing forces against each other, the ability to create distractions, and all the things we love about the Hitman games are here. Blood Money was the best at the old and limited system, Absolution is the worst at the new and improved system. The shift from one to another is very jarring.

My advice? Pick it up on a good sale.

Hitman: Absolution is totally worth playing, especially if you can get it for under ₹100 like I did. The graphics still look good even in 2026, and the stealth and combat mechanics are exactly what you would expect from a Hitman game.

The story and gameplay mechanics are excellent, but some missions can get frustrating because the game does not always clearly tell you what to do. In some difficult parts, I just gave up on stealth and started eliminating everyone.

If you want to play the game properly, you really need to learn the map layouts, disguise system, enemy paths, and how to approach each mission carefully. That is where the game becomes very satisfying.

One downside is that online features like Contracts mode are no longer active, which removes some replay value. Still, the main game is strong enough to enjoy on its own.

I still recommend Hitman: Absolution in 2026, especially for stealth game fans.
Plays a bit different to WoA, but still a good and underrated game. Contract achievements(which are normally impossible, especially on PS3) can be done using an easy guide.

Good game but it sucks that the 'Contracts' mode content is unavailable since the servers are no longer online (there's a way to self host the servers apparently but it looked like too much of a bother for me). The devs should rehost the servers once that James Bond money starts coming in.

Hitman Absolution - The walking to a door simulator, the thirst trap, and the black sheep of the Hitman Series. Hitman Absolution is 'absolutely' a mixed bag.

The Good:
- The gameplay is not actually that bad, after all, Hitman Absoultion was the game to introduce the modern system for Hitman: World of Assasination.
- The graphics look amazing, even better than the modern hitman games, with each level having a really dark atmosphere that really keeps you on your toes!
- Cutscenes look really well done.
- The music is really good, if you're into orchestral stuff, you might want to check out the OST.

The Bad:
- The level design ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ sucks, most of the levels are just walking to a door. There's only three levels in the game (The King of China Town, Hunter and Hunted, Fight Night) that are actually sandbox levels. The King of China Town and Hunter and Hunted reuse the same map too, just with different targets. Every other level either has you walking to a door or taking out an enemy the exact way that the developers wanted you too. The tutorial for the game is less linear than most missions, which is really saying something. You get deducted points for knocking people out too, which means if you want a high score, you have to do things down a scripted path exactly how the developers want.
- The game is a thirst trap, near the end of the game you're hunted down by multiple operatives from your former agency... And they're all dressed in super over the top sexy nun outfits... The game never explains why, the game doesn't even address their outfit, they're just there for a mission and then they're gone. Diana is also seen taking a shower before she gets killed in the background of the main menu.
- Disguises just straight up don't work in this game unless you have instinct, which if you don't know what that is, it's a limited resource that can burn out. Which means you can only use diguises during the start of a mission. Suit only is supposed to be a fun challenge, not the way that you're forced to play.

The Really Bad:
I saved the best for last, the story. Oh where do I even start? The game puts such a big focus that it's impossible to even ignore, after all, half the missions are walking to a door for narative purposes. (Spoilers) [spoiler] The plot follows agent 47 to is assigned by the ICA to kill his old handler, Diana. After the tutorial, when Agent 47 shoots Diana in the shower, Agent 47 decides he now owes Diana since he realized what he did was wrong. She told you to save her daughter, and that's exactly what Agent 47 tries to do. Throughout the game Agent 47 builds a strong connection with Diana's daughter. But guess what, Diana turns out to be alive at the end of the game, so the entire game was for nothing. [/spoiler] All of the characters are also super comedic and cartoony for a game that feels like it was meant to be taken seriously. Your intelligence guy is always covered in bird ♥♥♥♥, the main antagonist is some big industry texas CEO who hits on everyone and spares 47 multiple times by accident because he has the brain of a goldfish, and the targets are these absolute nobody goofballs that don't know what they're doing. All of those characters just so out of place compared to our introverted serious protagonist.

In conclusion, I would not reccommend unless you wanted to see how hitman evolved. But even then just by Blood Money, Contracts, or the modern trilogy. They're all better than this. The only reason I still play is because I only play the one or two missions that don't suck.
